title: Chapter 24: The Confession
content: The office is cold.

Not the cultivated chill of Sterling Capital's air system — that won't kick on for another hour. This is the dead cold of a building that hasn't warmed up yet, steel and glass still holding the night inside them. My footsteps made no sound on the carpet. The hallways were empty. The reception desk was dark. The whole fifty-second floor feels like a held breath.
I close the door behind me.

Julian's already here. Of course he is. He's always here — first one in, last one out, the man who runs on sleep denial and espresso and some internal combustion engine I've never quite understood. He stands when I enter, and his face does something I've never seen it do before. It can't decide. Relief and concern and fear all arriving at once, colliding in the set of his jaw, the furrow between his brows, the way his hands move half a step toward me and then stop.
"You came back."

The words land, and then they echo — not in this room, not in this timeline, but somewhere deeper. Paris. A cluttered apartment with morning light on the Seine. Julian B standing in a doorway with that same complicated face, saying those same three words. *Tu es revenue.* You came back.

For one sick, spinning moment, I hear them in stereo. Two Julians, same words, same relief, bleeding into each other across the walls of reality, and I have to press my palm flat against the cold glass of the nearest bookshelf just to remember which world I'm standing in.
This one. Timeline A. The one with the sharp edges and the original Julian, who is looking at me like I might shatter or disappear.
I might do both.
"I need to tell you something," I say, and my voice sounds thin in the pre-dawn quiet. "And you're going to think I've lost my mind."
He doesn't move. Doesn't sit back down behind that massive desk, doesn't gesture me toward a chair. Just stands there, watching me with those dark, careful eyes, waiting.
So I tell him.
Everything.

No half-measures this time. No strategic omissions, no careful curations of truth. I take the whole impossible mess of it and lay it at his feet like something broken I've been carrying in my arms for too long.

The Fork app — how it appeared on my phone one night with no download history, no developer name, just that sleek bifurcating icon and the question that started all of this. The three timelines — A, B, C — each one splitting from a single moment, each one as real as the others. The three versions of him — Julian A, the Wall Street titan; Julian B, the Parisian gallerist; Julian C, the fisherman in Kinsale — each one the same soul in different skin, each one pulling at me in different ways.

I tell him about Cork. The merger that started as whispers and wrong memories, the way the timelines have been collapsing into each other, the composite entity forming at the intersection. I tell him about the integrity percentage — how it started in the eighties and has been dropping like a stone.
I pull out my phone and show him.
The number glows on the screen, small and white and definitive. Below it, the fork icon pulses — slower now, like a heartbeat running out of strength.
His face doesn't change.

That's the part I can't get over. I just told him that reality is fracturing, that there are three of him, that some kind of merged consciousness is waking up in the space between worlds, and his expression stays exactly the same. Calm. Attentive. Like I'm presenting a quarterly earnings report.

He doesn't interrupt. Doesn't shake his head. Doesn't do the thing every rational person would do, which is look at me like I've finally cracked under the pressure of the merger and need to be gently escorted to a hospital.
When I finish, there's silence.

The kind of silence that has weight. Manhattan is starting to murmur outside — the first subway rumble of the morning, a distant siren threading between buildings — but in here, nothing. Just the two of us and the cold and the gray light beginning to seep through the floor-to-ceiling windows behind him.
Then he says, "I believe you."
Not *that's impossible.* Not *are you sure.* Not *have you been sleeping?* Not even a pause to process.
Just: I believe you.
My throat closes. "Why?" The word comes out cracked, barely there. "Why would you believe something like that? It's — it's insane, Julian. It's objectively insane."

He doesn't answer right away. Instead, he walks to the window, and I watch the gray morning light find the edges of him — the broad lines of his shoulders, the disciplined set of his spine, the way his reflection ghosts against the Manhattan skyline. The city is all charcoal and silver out there, the buildings like teeth in a half-open mouth, not quite awake, not quite asleep.
"Because I've been dreaming about places I've never been," he says quietly. To the window, not to me. Like it's easier to confess to the skyline.
My pulse spikes. "What places?"

"A gallery in Paris with gold light. The walls were white and the light coming through the windows was — it was like honey. I could smell turpentine and old wood." He pauses. "There was a painting of the sea. You were standing in front of it, and you had something on your fingers — ink or paint. You kept touching the frame like you were reading it."
He turns to look at me, and his eyes are bright with something raw. Something that's been hiding.

"A fishing boat at dawn. Cold. Wet. I could feel the spray on my face, and the boat was rocking, and my hands — my hands were rough. Callused. They don't look like that." He holds them up, these smooth, manicured hands that have never hauled a net. "But in the dream, they did."
My breath catches. Julian C's hands. Julian C's boat.

"And a cat café," he continues, and his voice breaks slightly on the word *cat*, like even he can hear how absurd it is and can't stop. "Somewhere that smells like the sea and coffee and — and animal fur. There were cats everywhere, and you were laughing, and I was sitting across from you with a book I wasn't reading because I couldn't—"
He stops. Swallows.
"Because I couldn't stop looking at you."
The room is too quiet. I can hear my own heartbeat in my ears, in my fingertips, in the hollow of my throat.
"And every single dream," he says, "you were there."
I can't move. I can barely breathe. Because I understand what he's telling me, and it changes everything.
The bleed isn't one-way.

I've been so focused on what the Fork app is doing to me — the glitching between timelines, the memories that don't belong to me, the way I keep slipping — that I never stopped to think about what was happening on the other end. The merge works both ways. Julian A has been receiving memories too. His other selves' lives have been leaking into his sleep, dripping through the walls of reality, and he's been carrying that alone just like I have.
For weeks.
Possibly longer.
"Julian—"

He's already moving. Not fast — deliberate. Each step is measured, intentional, like he's been waiting to take this walk for a long time and wants to remember every inch of it. The distance between us closes. Ten feet. Six. Three.

"I've been trying to understand why I couldn't stop thinking about you," he says. Another step. "I told myself it was professional interest. You were the best analyst I'd ever worked with. That had to be it." Another step. "Then I told myself it was attraction. You're — you're beautiful, and you're brilliant, and those things together are—"
He stops directly in front of me. Close enough that I can feel the warmth radiating off him, close enough that if I swayed forward even an inch, I'd be against his chest.

"But it was never either of those things," he says, and his voice is raw, stripped of every bit of the polished executive. "It was... gravity. Like something was pulling me toward you that had nothing to do with logic or want. Like I'd already chosen you before I knew there was a choice."
His hand rises — slow, so slow, giving me every chance to pull back — and touches my jaw. His fingers are warm against my cold skin. I can feel the slight tremor in them.
"I think I've been falling in love with you across three worlds," he says, "and I didn't know it."
The dam breaks.

Not the stoic crack from before — not the single tear I let slip in Paris or the tight-throated composure I practiced in the bathroom mirror this morning. This is collapse. This is every wall I've built since the Fork app first appeared, every moment of isolation and confusion and screaming-into-the-void loneliness, all of it pouring out of me in a single catastrophic flood.

Tears. Shaking. The whole mess of it — ugly and loud and I can't stop it and I don't even try. I'm sobbing in Julian Vance's office before the sun is fully up, and I can taste the salt of my own tears, and my breath is hitching like a broken engine, and I am so tired of being strong about this.

Because I've been holding it alone. Not just the timeline stuff — though God, yes, that, the weight of knowing that reality itself is fraying and I'm the only one who can see the threads. But also the love of it. The impossible, unbearable love of three men who share a face and a soul, and the certainty that I could reach for any one of them and find the same depth looking back at me.
Someone knows now. Someone believes me. Someone is standing right in front of me, close enough that I can feel the heat of him through my clothes, saying the thing I was most terrified to hope for.
"I don't know how to save any of this," I choke out. The words are water-logged, thick with crying. "I don't know how to fix the timelines or stop the collapse or— or choose—"
His hands find my face.

Both of them. Cradling my jaw, tilting my chin up, his thumbs brushing the wet from my cheeks. It's the same gesture Julian B used in Paris — the same cradle, the same tenderness, the same way of holding someone like they're precious and breakable and worth being gentle with.
Same gesture, different man, same soul.
"You don't have to fix everything," he says. His voice is steady. Unshakable. A lighthouse in a storm of my own making. "You just have to let me help."

I want to. God, I want to. I want to sink into the solid warmth of him and let someone else carry this for five minutes, let someone else figure out the impossible math of three collapsing timelines and a world that won't hold together. I want to be the person who gets rescued instead of the person doing the rescuing.
But the universe, as always, has impeccable timing.
My phone buzzes.

The vibration is sharp against my hip — insistent, undeniable. I know what it is before I pull it out of my pocket. I know the way the Fork app announces itself, that low thrum of wrongness that makes my teeth ache.
INTEGRITY: 7%
Down two points in the space of a conversation. The number blinks at me, white on black, clinical and final.
Then something new happens.
The screen flickers — not the usual glitch-stutter, but a deliberate shift. And text appears. Not icons. Not percentages. Words.
The first words the Fork app has ever spoken to me directly.
CHOOSE ONE. STAY. OR LOSE ALL.
I stare at it. The letters don't move. They don't pulse. They just sit there, plain and unadorned, the app finally saying what it's been meaning to say all along.
Choose one timeline. Preserve it. The other two will dissolve — not freeze, not archive, not fade gently into memory. *Dissolve*. Everything and everyone in them, unmade. Gone.

Julian B's warm apartment with its cluttered bookshelves and the way morning light turns the Seine to gold. His hands that shake when he's nervous. His *reste* — stay — whispered like a prayer against my hair.

Julian C's boat rocking in the harbor at dawn, his arm around my shoulders, the smell of salt and engine oil. His rough palms and his quiet voice and the way he said *I don't want to do this alone anymore*, like it cost him everything to admit.

Julian A, standing in front of me right now, his hands still cupping my face, his thumbs still brushing my tears away, the gray Manhattan light behind him and the whole of Sterling Capital sleeping around us, unaware that the world is ending. This Julian, who dreamed of places he'd never been and believed the impossible and said *gravity* like it was the only word that made sense.
The same soul. Three different lives. And I have to pick one.

I look at him — at Julian A, at this Julian, the one who's real and present and warm and here — and I think of the others, who are just as real, just as present, just as impossibly dear to me in their own timelines.
How can I choose?
How can I not?
His hands are still on my face. I can feel his pulse in his fingertips, or maybe that's my own pulse, or maybe it's the same pulse, the same heartbeat counting down in three worlds at once.
The phone screen glows in my peripheral vision. CHOOSE ONE. STAY. OR LOSE ALL.
Seven percent.
And I don't have forever.
